summary: The notebook demonstrates a complete inference pipeline for generating SVG illustrations from text prompts by combining Stable Diffusion image synthesis with a custom raster-to-vector conversion algorithm. It evaluates candidate SVGs locally using a modified competition metric that blends a VQA-based fidelity score (via PaliGemma) and a CLIP-based aesthetic score, selecting the highest-scoring output after multiple generation attempts. The author packages the workflow into a reusable `Model` class and includes detailed runtime profiling to estimate submission feasibility. Readers should understand how to leverage diffusion models for vector graphics generation, implement custom metric evaluation locally, and optimize generation parameters for time-constrained inference.
competition_flow: Loads text descriptions from a CSV, generates candidate images via Stable Diffusion, converts them to SVGs using a contour-based algorithm, scores them locally with a VQA + aesthetic metric, and returns the best SVG for submission.
insights: Iterative generation with multiple attempts per prompt reliably improves final metric scores within strict time constraints.
A weighted harmonic mean (beta=2.0) of VQA fidelity and CLIP aesthetic scores closely approximates the competition's official metric.
Disabling the Stable Diffusion safety checker and using half-precision significantly reduces inference latency.
Contour simplification and color quantization are necessary to keep generated SVGs within file size limits.
Pre-defined default SVGs can serve as effective fallback candidates when diffusion generation fails or scores poorly.
